clusters.title: Urban living may shield against stroke, longitudinal study finds
clusters.summary: While urban life is often associated with stress and pollution, living in more intensely developed neighborhoods may actually protect against stroke, a new University of Michigan study suggests. The research published in Cities & Health tracked more than 25,000 adults across the United States for over a decade, and found that residents in areas with higher levels of development, characterized by more buildings, sidewalks and infrastructure, had a 2.5% lower risk of suffering a first-time stroke compared to residents in less developed areas.

items.title: Urban living may shield against stroke, longitudinal study finds
items.description: While urban life is often associated with stress and pollution, living in more intensely developed neighborhoods may actually protect against stroke, a new University of Michigan study suggests. The research published in Cities & Health tracked more than 25,000 adults across the United States for over a decade, and found that residents in areas with higher levels of development, characterized by more buildings, sidewalks and infrastructure, had a 2.5% lower risk of suffering a first-time stroke compared to residents in less developed areas.

clusters.title: Popular pre-workout supplements linked to dangerous sleep loss
clusters.summary: Pre-workout supplements promising extra energy for workouts may come with a hidden cost: severely reduced sleep. A study of people aged 16–30 found users were more than twice as likely to sleep five hours or less per night. Many of these products pack huge doses of caffeine and stimulants that can linger for hours.

items.title: Popular pre-workout supplements linked to dangerous sleep loss
items.description: Pre-workout supplements promising extra energy for workouts may come with a hidden cost: severely reduced sleep. A study of people aged 16–30 found users were more than twice as likely to sleep five hours or less per night. Many of these products pack huge doses of caffeine and stimulants that can linger for hours. Researchers say the findings raise concerns about the impact on young people’s health and development.

clusters.title: Scientists reveal why human language isn’t like computer code
clusters.summary: Human language may seem messy and inefficient compared to the ultra-compact strings of ones and zeros used by computers—but our brains actually prefer it that way. New research reveals that while digital-style encoding could theoretically compress information more tightly, it would demand far more mental effort from both speaker and listener. Instead, language is built around familiar words and predictable patterns that reflect our real-world experiences, allowing the brain to constantly anticipate what comes next and narrow down meaning step by step.

items.title: Scientists reveal why human language isn’t like computer code
items.description: Human language may seem messy and inefficient compared to the ultra-compact strings of ones and zeros used by computers—but our brains actually prefer it that way. New research reveals that while digital-style encoding could theoretically compress information more tightly, it would demand far more mental effort from both speaker and listener. Instead, language is built around familiar words and predictable patterns that reflect our real-world experiences, allowing the brain to constantly anticipate what comes next and narrow down meaning step by step.

clusters.title: Scientists discover molecule that stops aggressive breast cancer in its tracks
clusters.summary: Scientists at Oregon Health & Science University have developed a new molecule that could open the door to treating triple-negative breast cancer, one of the most aggressive and difficult-to-treat forms of the disease. The compound, called SU212, targets and disables a key enzyme that cancer cells rely on to fuel their growth. In tests using humanized mouse models, the molecule caused tumors to shrink and slowed the spread of cancer by forcing the enzyme to break down.

items.title: Scientists discover molecule that stops aggressive breast cancer in its tracks
items.description: Scientists at Oregon Health & Science University have developed a new molecule that could open the door to treating triple-negative breast cancer, one of the most aggressive and difficult-to-treat forms of the disease. The compound, called SU212, targets and disables a key enzyme that cancer cells rely on to fuel their growth. In tests using humanized mouse models, the molecule caused tumors to shrink and slowed the spread of cancer by forcing the enzyme to break down.

clusters.title: Cannabis study finds THC can create false memories
clusters.summary: THC doesn’t just blur memories—it can create new ones that never happened. In a controlled experiment, cannabis users were much more likely to recall words that were never shown and struggled with tasks like remembering to do something later. Researchers found that THC disrupted many different memory systems at once.

items.title: Cannabis study finds THC can create false memories
items.description: THC doesn’t just blur memories—it can create new ones that never happened. In a controlled experiment, cannabis users were much more likely to recall words that were never shown and struggled with tasks like remembering to do something later. Researchers found that THC disrupted many different memory systems at once. Surprisingly, moderate doses caused memory problems similar to higher doses.

clusters.title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
clusters.summary: A new experimental drug is showing remarkable promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic form of epilepsy. In clinical trials, the treatment zorevunersen cut seizures by as much as 91% while also improving quality of life for many patients. The therapy works by boosting the function of a key gene involved in nerve cell signaling.

items.title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
items.description: A new experimental drug is showing remarkable promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic form of epilepsy. In clinical trials, the treatment zorevunersen cut seizures by as much as 91% while also improving quality of life for many patients. The therapy works by boosting the function of a key gene involved in nerve cell signaling. Encouraging results have led researchers to launch a larger Phase 3 trial.

clusters.title: Common pesticide may more than double Parkinson’s disease risk
clusters.summary: A new UCLA Health study suggests that long-term exposure to the pesticide chlorpyrifos may dramatically raise the risk of Parkinson’s disease. Researchers found that people living in areas with sustained exposure had more than 2.5 times the likelihood of developing the disorder. Lab experiments reinforced the finding: animals exposed to the chemical developed movement problems, lost dopamine-producing neurons, and showed the same toxic protein buildup seen in Parkinson’s patients.

items.title: Common pesticide may more than double Parkinson’s disease risk
items.description: A new UCLA Health study suggests that long-term exposure to the pesticide chlorpyrifos may dramatically raise the risk of Parkinson’s disease. Researchers found that people living in areas with sustained exposure had more than 2.5 times the likelihood of developing the disorder. Lab experiments reinforced the finding: animals exposed to the chemical developed movement problems, lost dopamine-producing neurons, and showed the same toxic protein buildup seen in Parkinson’s patients.

clusters.title: Building muscle strength may help prevent depression, especially in women
clusters.summary: A new study suggests that building muscle strength could play a direct role in reducing depression risk, particularly in women. Researchers found no evidence that aerobic fitness offers the same mental health benefits.

items.title: Building muscle strength may help prevent depression, especially in women
items.description: A new study suggests that building muscle strength could play a direct role in reducing depression risk, particularly in women. Researchers found no evidence that aerobic fitness offers the same mental health benefits.

clusters.title: New brain stimulation approach could treat depression in just 5 days
clusters.summary: A weeklong, high-intensity version of TMS may work nearly as well as the standard six-week treatment for depression. In a UCLA study, patients who received five sessions a day for five days experienced meaningful symptom relief comparable to those on the traditional schedule. Some who didn’t improve immediately showed strong gains weeks later.

items.title: New brain stimulation approach could treat depression in just 5 days
items.description: A weeklong, high-intensity version of TMS may work nearly as well as the standard six-week treatment for depression. In a UCLA study, patients who received five sessions a day for five days experienced meaningful symptom relief comparable to those on the traditional schedule. Some who didn’t improve immediately showed strong gains weeks later. The findings hint at a faster, more accessible path to recovery.

clusters.title: Tracking sleep with an app? Why insomnia sufferers may feel worse, not better
clusters.summary: The increasing availability of sleep monitoring apps, and rising interest in sleep health, has led to a sharp increase in people tracking their rest. But these apps might not give people an accurate image of their sleep, and they could be making it harder for people to rest.

items.title: Tracking sleep with an app? Why insomnia sufferers may feel worse, not better
items.description: The increasing availability of sleep monitoring apps, and rising interest in sleep health, has led to a sharp increase in people tracking their rest. But these apps might not give people an accurate image of their sleep, and they could be making it harder for people to rest.

clusters.title: Study finds two types of colon polyps can raise bowel cancer risk fivefold
clusters.summary: Researchers studying over 8,400 colonoscopies discovered that having both adenomas and serrated polyps in the bowel can raise the risk of serious precancerous changes by up to five times. These two polyp types may represent separate cancer pathways that can occur at the same time. Nearly half of patients with serrated polyps also had adenomas, making this high-risk combination more common than expected.

items.title: Study finds two types of colon polyps can raise bowel cancer risk fivefold
items.description: Researchers studying over 8,400 colonoscopies discovered that having both adenomas and serrated polyps in the bowel can raise the risk of serious precancerous changes by up to five times. These two polyp types may represent separate cancer pathways that can occur at the same time. Nearly half of patients with serrated polyps also had adenomas, making this high-risk combination more common than expected. The results emphasize the importance of early detection and regular colonoscopy monitoring.

clusters.title: Physical activity improves work ability: Study shows lifelong influence from childhood to the end of career
clusters.summary: A study conducted at the University of Jyväskylä shows that regular leisure-time physical activity started at a young age prevents a decrease in work ability at the end of a career. The result is societally significant, as productivity losses due to reduced work ability cost billions of euros annually.

items.title: Physical activity improves work ability: Study shows lifelong influence from childhood to the end of career

The result is societally significant, as productivity losses due to reduced work ability cost billions of euros annually.

clusters.title: Largest study of its kind tests hydration strategy for kidney stones
clusters.summary: Kidney stones can cause some of the most intense pain people ever experience, affecting daily life and leading many to hospital emergency visits. It affects one in 11 people in the U.S., and almost half will experience a recurrence. A major new study from the Urinary Stone Disease Research Network, coordinated by the Duke Clinical Research Institute, tested whether a behavioral program could help people drink enough fluids to prevent stones from coming back.

items.title: Largest study of its kind tests hydration strategy for kidney stones

It affects one in 11 people in the U.S., and almost half will experience a recurrence. clusters.title: First test of a new neuroscience theory shows how smart brains coordinate information
clusters.summary: By scanning brains while people actively took intelligence tests, neuroscientists discovered that high test scores are linked to highly flexible, diverse neural networks. The findings offer the first physical evidence for a new theory of human intelligence.

items.title: First test of a new neuroscience theory shows how smart brains coordinate information
items.description: By scanning brains while people actively took intelligence tests, neuroscientists discovered that high test scores are linked to highly flexible, diverse neural networks. The findings offer the first physical evidence for a new theory of human intelligence.

clusters.title: One in three people carry this brain parasite but the body has a kill switch
clusters.summary: A parasite that may already be hiding in your brain has a shocking survival trick: it can infect the very immune cells sent to destroy it. Yet most people never get sick, and new research from UVA Health reveals why. Scientists discovered that when Toxoplasma gondii invades CD8+ T cells — key defenders of the immune system — those cells can trigger a self-destruct mechanism powered by an enzyme called caspase-8.

items.title: One in three people carry this brain parasite but the body has a kill switch
items.description: A parasite that may already be hiding in your brain has a shocking survival trick: it can infect the very immune cells sent to destroy it. Yet most people never get sick, and new research from UVA Health reveals why. Scientists discovered that when Toxoplasma gondii invades CD8+ T cells — key defenders of the immune system — those cells can trigger a self-destruct mechanism powered by an enzyme called caspase-8. By sacrificing themselves, the infected cells also wipe out the parasite inside them.

clusters.title: This virus therapy supercharges the immune system against brain cancer
clusters.summary: Scientists have found a way to make one of the most aggressive brain tumors vulnerable to the immune system. A single injection of a modified virus can invade glioblastoma, kill cancer cells, and summon immune fighters deep into the tumor. These immune cells persist and attack, which was linked to longer survival in patients.

items.title: This virus therapy supercharges the immune system against brain cancer
items.description: Scientists have found a way to make one of the most aggressive brain tumors vulnerable to the immune system. A single injection of a modified virus can invade glioblastoma, kill cancer cells, and summon immune fighters deep into the tumor. These immune cells persist and attack, which was linked to longer survival in patients.

clusters.title: A lab mistake at Cambridge reveals a powerful new way to modify drug molecules
clusters.summary: Cambridge scientists have discovered a light-powered chemical reaction that lets researchers modify complex drug molecules at the final stages of development. Unlike traditional methods that rely on toxic chemicals and harsh conditions, the new approach uses an LED lamp to create essential carbon–carbon bonds under mild conditions. This could make drug discovery faster and more environmentally friendly.

items.title: A lab mistake at Cambridge reveals a powerful new way to modify drug molecules
items.description: Cambridge scientists have discovered a light-powered chemical reaction that lets researchers modify complex drug molecules at the final stages of development. Unlike traditional methods that rely on toxic chemicals and harsh conditions, the new approach uses an LED lamp to create essential carbon–carbon bonds under mild conditions. This could make drug discovery faster and more environmentally friendly. The breakthrough was uncovered unexpectedly during a failed laboratory experiment.

clusters.title: The psychological reason we judge groups much more harshly than individuals
clusters.summary: New research reveals a fascinating hierarchy in human judgment: we see ourselves as exceptionally moral and give individual strangers the benefit of the doubt, yet we view society and groups as falling short.

items.title: The psychological reason we judge groups much more harshly than individuals
items.description: New research reveals a fascinating hierarchy in human judgment: we see ourselves as exceptionally moral and give individual strangers the benefit of the doubt, yet we view society and groups as falling short.

clusters.title: MRI study finds Down syndrome brain lesions can shrink over time
clusters.summary: What has long been interpreted as permanent and irreversible vascular damage may not be exclusively so. In people with Down syndrome—one of the most robust populations for studying Alzheimer's disease due to the near-universal presence of the characteristic proteinopathies of this dementia from the age of 40—some lesions visible on magnetic resonance imaging do not follow a linear course.

items.title: MRI study finds Down syndrome brain lesions can shrink over time
items.description: What has long been interpreted as permanent and irreversible vascular damage may not be exclusively so. In people with Down syndrome—one of the most robust populations for studying Alzheimer's disease due to the near-universal presence of the characteristic proteinopathies of this dementia from the age of 40—some lesions visible on magnetic resonance imaging do not follow a linear course.

clusters.title: Scientists discover the brain protein that drives cocaine relapse
clusters.summary: Cocaine addiction isn’t simply a failure of willpower — it’s the result of lasting biological changes in the brain. Researchers at Michigan State University discovered that repeated cocaine use rewires communication between the brain’s reward system and the hippocampus, the region responsible for memory. A protein called DeltaFosB builds up with continued drug use and acts like a genetic switch, altering how neurons function and strengthening the brain’s drive to seek cocaine.
